Description: Optimal average power for Indium oxide removal without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength providing good absorption in Indium oxide layer
Beam spot diameter for precise oxide removal
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage
Fluence threshold for Indium oxide ablation
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled oxide ablation
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning
Recommended number of passes for complete oxide removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform coverage

Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in metal cleaning; lower end for delicate soft metals like Indium to avoid melting (mp 156.6°C). Recommended start conservatively low. Source: IPG Photonics specs for cleaning heads; conservative for Indium's low thermal resistance (similar to Al studies in Opt. Lasers Eng. 2018).

Effective for contamination removal on reflective metals; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., oxides), faster for light. Recommended balances speed and fluence control. Source: Industry standards from Laserax manuals; adapted for Indium's high reflectivity reducing absorption.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning with minimal HAZ on soft metals; picosecond (ps) for ultra-precision but overkill for Indium. Avoids excessive heat buildup due to Indium's conductivity (82 W/m·K). Source: Laser physics in J. Appl. Phys. 2015 on ns ablation thresholds for low-mp metals.

Typical for industrial cleaning; moderate rate prevents heat accumulation on heat-sensitive Indium while enabling efficient coverage. Higher rates increase speed but risk thermal damage. Source: Academic lit. in Appl. Surf. Sci. 2020 on pulse train effects for metals.

Fluence below Indium's ablation threshold (~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to reflectivity >90%); safe for contaminant removal without substrate melting. Conservative to account for variability in contamination. Source: Ablation studies in Phys. Procedia 2013 for soft metals; estimated for Indium.

Smaller for precise surface prep on Indium components (e.g., electronics); larger for broad areas. Balances fluence and coverage. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent Inc. cleaning systems; suitable for fine Indium features.

Multi-pass for thorough removal of layered contamination without single-pass overload; single pass for light cleaning. Recommended for uniform Indium surface prep. Source: Industry case studies in J. Laser Appl. 2019 on metal oxide removal.

Ensures uniform coverage on irregular Indium surfaces; 50% optimal for efficiency without excessive exposure. Higher for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization in Opt. Laser Technol. 2017 for scanned laser cleaning.
